speaker: interviewer
text: It's May 3, 1995. We are about to begin tape three in an interview with Mrs. Lilly Tykocinski. OK. So when we left off, it was roughly Christmas time, 1943. And your two sisters had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: --taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was selected to work in the union, in the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were moved from the quarantine from block 20 to block 2. This was a second field, a different field, you know. And we went to work-- every day, 5 o'clock. They dressed us a little bit more human. You know, we had to wear shoes, and a uniform dress, and the stripes, and a jacket. And we walked every day to work. We worked. I worked. They selected me to sit at the children's table. They made a children's table. So there were like 14, 15 children-- a blond, a redhead, a brunette, a Hungarian, a Litvak, a Belgian, Polish, German, Jew-- from each country, one child. I don't know why it was-- maybe just to show off us. And we work. We were testing some kind of a device. We didn't even know what we are doing. You know, they told us with a device to test this opening. And we tested. Either it's good or it's [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went back to the barracks and every day to work. And there were selections. The food was still scarce. The selections didn't stop. And we had a kapo. She was a German-- also a haftling-- German. Either she was a prostitute or something-- that she was incarcerated. And she was in Auschwitz, but she was in charge of us. So there were like 700 Jews and 100 Germans walking every day to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was-- was she Jewish, this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wasn't Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were German, real Germans. But they were prostitutes, or political, or some kind that they did something bad that they set in Auschwitz. So we walked every day with her, rain or shine, every day in and every day out. There were selections coming home from work or going to work. One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not every day, every second day, twice a week, you know. We were working. We were productive people. They were-- the-- the factory was made like a temporary thing-- made from glass roof and walls. There were 1/2 divided for women, 1/2 for men. And there was night shift and day shift. I worked on the day shift. There were-- we were 700 women going to work every day, and probably the same amount of men-- the men who lived in the-- we saw the men. We couldn't speak to them, what-- but we-- we looked at them, you know, through the-- through the gates, because they were separate. And we walked home one day. And I had swollen legs, because I was still sick. My legs were completely-- I was thin, and my legs were completely swollen. And we came into work. They said it was too early. So they made a selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told us to run like from there, to there-- to run. I couldn't run. I was walking. My-- my legs were stiff, so I couldn't run. So they took my number to-- to send me back to the block, that I am not good if I cannot run.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were maybe 10 girls with me. They all went out with the selection. So we were standing there and waiting to be sent back home. This was on a Monday. Because Sunday, we had potatoes-- a potato and the skin. And the SS men that brought us takes us back into the barracks. So going back home, I says, listen. I'm going to die anyhow, let me eat up whatever I-- I have left-- a piece of bread. I have a potato-- and whatever I had left in that little bag. Let me eat. I'm not-- may not be hungry if I'm going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ate everything up. And I'm going back to the block. We came in the block. All right. So why did you back-- get back? I says, we were selected to go back. So he says, go to your beds.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sitting, and sitting, and sitting. Nothing happened. The day passed by. The night passed by. They says, don't go to work. Wait another day or two. In another day or two, you go back to work. Nobody called for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened? The kapo forgot to give the numbers to the SS men. She was in love with him. So she talked so much to him, she forgot to give him the numbers. So they didn't know what's happening. So that's why the selection-- otherwise, they-- they would put us to block 10, and we would be gone. How-- how did you find out this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after two, three days, we went back to work. And the master that we worked for was a civilian. He asked, where is she? What happened to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said, she-- she's gone. She's gone. He says, my God. He couldn't do anything, because they couldn't understand. Because she was a civilian that work. He knew about the-- this job. So that-- this was one selection. This was one lucky day for me. The-- the second lucky day was when I had the swollen legs, when I came back at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The SS man put my-- the fingers in. There was a hole, you know, from the swelling. So he was-- I was going already to the left, to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a secretary with him, a Czechoslovakian. She says, 'Come back. Do you drink lots of tea or-- or water?' And she says like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, yes. 'You shouldn't do that anymore.' And she says, 'You see, Herr Obersturmbannfuhrer, she-- from drinking the tea and water, she has the swollen legs.' They took me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked out. This was-- this was-- this was two or three. I had lucky breaks like that. And-- [LAUGHS] --this is not-- this is not funny, but everybody kissed me and hugged me that I-- that I went through this selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we walked out, so-- oh, this was later. I tell you why-- how did I find out. The Americans were flying through Germany already through-- that was a air, you know, like, how do you call it? A sortie? They were bombing.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bombed. They wanted bomb. They were going by, so we had to lay down flat on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, they told us not to lay down under the table, to go in in the sauna. In the factory was a sauna with showers that the kapos, the German women, had-- could bathe. And we all were laying on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kapo that was laying next to me, she fainted. She was so scared, because the jets were coming down-- the bombers. There were no bombs, but we were scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she got so scared that she fainted. And I was next to her. So I, for my satisfaction, I gave her one on one side and on the other side to wake her up, to see if she's alive. So she came to. And she says, look. From all my friends, the little Jew is next to me. She-- she revived me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since, then I became her 'pupu'-- her pupil. I was a, in German, they say, [GERMAN]. And she treated me like I never was treated before. First of all, I was going with her by the hand in the first row with the Germans, the first 100. So she was holding my whole-- hand when we walked out from the factory, marching out. You know, we-- we had to sing. The Germans were singing songs to march out. And then she gave me soap to take a shower. And she was-- she was so thankful that I saved her life, that I was with her when nobody was there-- that she tried to be very nice to me. And once, we were talking on a Sunday, privately, you know, because Sunday we didn't work. And she asked me to come. Maybe she'll give me something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, Maria, do you remember this incident that I was selected to go back to block? So she says, and I forgot to give the numbers. And she told me that. So you see, you are meant to survive, to be here with me now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she kept me through all the time. You know, she allowed me to take a shower in the sauna. And whatever she had something left, she gave to me. She gave me a nightgown, which we never had to sleep. We-- and then in 1944, in the summer, they-- they transferred us into Auschwitz, from Birkenau. This was all in Birkenau. They moved the union, the whole commando, into Auschwitz, into four blocks-- separate, clean. We had already-- we-- we slept a little bit more comfortable-- not with linen, God forbid, but a little bit more comfortable. We had a little bit more food, you know. Everybody who survived already, you became a little bit a mensch. And we worked every day, because the people in the factory needed us. And there were always inspection. They were coming, the generals. And they're looking at the Jews-- what they are doing. And they came to the table from the children-- those-- our children here. They took pictures probably. And they always showed off with the-- with the children. You know, each one was-- beautiful young girls. And so-- oh, my husband is coming. So everybody-- you know. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't know where I was. Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [DRINKING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took us to Auschwitz. And we stayed in there in the four blocks-- very comfortable. There was one block that was separated for prostitutes. And we didn't know. But someday, they came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Though they say they are prostitutes for the Germans. I don't know if they were Jewish or Jewish girls. We didn't-- we didn't discuss it with them. And one block was closed off with-- with wires-- that we couldn't get in. This was the experiment block-- that they did all the experiments on the girls. That-- that time, we found out that there were girls that they did experiments on them. Because through the-- through the gates, we could talk about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of them told us what they did. Beautiful-- the nicest, youngest girls-- that they were-- they were doing experiments, the Germans, on them. And they were separated in a separate block. So from Auschwitz, we went also every day to work. But it was a little bit-- there was not-- there were no selections-- almost none. You know, whoever was sick, there was a doctor you could go to-- revere-- to the-- to the sick doctor. Or they-- they took you away. But it was already '44, and they said that they don't burn the people anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't read any papers. We didn't-- I didn't discuss politics then. Everybody wanted to survive the best way he knows how-- and so did we. And that's what-- this was the time when I was friendly with this kapo. And she helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with her at this point, when you moved from Birkenau to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they all moved. The Germans moved, too, yeah. Because the Germans worked in the factory, too. They were all like with-- with armband. They were watching over us. They gave them a little better jobs than we had. And this was the year '44, when the people were planning to-- to bomb the crematorium to-- to, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was stealing and taking out the powder from the powder rooms. And they giving to the men. This was working-- that I didn't know about it. And then the crematorium exploded one. And they hanged the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you-- you were in Auschwitz when the crematoria exploded? Do you remember that actually happening that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. We went to work in the morning and came back at night. And at night, you went to bed. You couldn't sleep. It was winter when we walked to work in snow, in rain. And my feet were wet, whatever I possessed. You had no place where to dry it. You had to dry the-- the-- the socks and the stockings, whatever you had on your feet. And if you came to work, they dried on your feet again, because there is no place that you get-- can get undressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And snow in Europe was high-- that we walked in-- till the knee-high, and snow every day, in and out there. And the winters were long and cold. So who bothered to find out what's going on? And this was a secret. So nobody talked about it. Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember approximately what month it happened in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember, but I think it was in the summer. It was a time that when they transferred us to Auschwitz, you know, in this camp. I really don't know. But later on, I found out. But what about-- I didn't bother with months, with dates, what's happening. I just wanted to-- to see, to survive, or to see what my death is going to be. This was my concern, and not to-- to-- to have enough to eat, to survive. You know, so maybe older people were interested what's going on. Maybe they were plotting something-- but not me. I wasn't involved with anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Just-- just one quick question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This kapo, Maria, you said her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hapenhofer-- Hapenhofer. She was from Vienna. I was looking for her in Vienna after the war, and we couldn't find her. Yeah. But to me, she was beating people as much as she could. She was beating a lot. But to me, she was good. And I, you know, I was lucky in this-- in this respect. This was my luck, probably. But she remembered.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the only thing that I-- that I-- maybe God wanted it that way. Who knows? That I had to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they get blind. They don't hear. They don't see. And you survive. You don't know the reasons why.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked in Auschwitz every day for a whole full year. And the selections were going on every day, every week, once a week, twice a week. Who counted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time we-- we went home, they said, something is going on on the block. One day, we walked out with this kapo. And we started marching. And I fell. I tripped. Because my-- I wasn't so-- you know, my feet weren't so steady. I didn't look so good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tripped. And like everybody almost fell over me, because if you march up with one step, so everybody marched with the same step for countdown. So a woman from the back, a Jewish woman, said, [NON-ENGLISH]. You understand what it means? What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fallen, God helps. Whoever fells, God helps. And we came to the block. And it was such a selection. We didn't know. They looked for everything. They got blind. I went through. I had although swollen legs, and I was thin. I went through with this selection, too. And this woman-- and this voice comes always back to me. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I heard that this woman survived. And she lived with her daughter. She has a daughter mine age, in Paris. She's from Warsaw. And now, to me, she was that time, an old lady. She was a mother of a child my age. So to me, she was an old lady. But this was most of the-- of the-- of the selections. And then by the end of the year-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --December?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Take a look. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then in January, 1945, January 6th, when they caught those people that set out the-- the crematorium, that they burned down, four girls were hanged in Auschwitz-- were four from our girls that work with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that day, we walked home from the factory. Somehow, they gave us a special ration of bread in the factory. And I says, what's happening? Is the war coming to an end that we getting extra food?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came in to the-- to the barracks. There were all the people out already-- the night shift and the rest of the day shift. And we all stood there. And there was-- those hanging things were ready. And right in front of me, they opened up the road for the people to march through. And the four girls marched through me. And I said to her, head high. And she picked up her head. This is the girl-- one of the girls. And they walked them over. And they hanged them in front of us. The whole factory was standing at that-- few hundred people, day and night, maybe 1,500 people. The day shift and the night shift had to be present to watch the hanging of those four girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of them personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The two sisters-- one sister is alive. Not really personally, but we talked, you know. You-- you didn't know anybody really by the name, by the number, or from what town are you. But those two sisters, I knew-- beautiful girls. So one was hanged. And the other sister we were comforting, because she was fainting. She was crying, because she lost her-- her sister. And they hang her at that time. This was January 6th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans make some kind of announcement to you when they hung these four girls, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They just want us to see what happen to people, what they're doing, things like that. They tortured them before. They were sitting in a bunker. They beat them to say-- they should say who-- who did they give the powder? Because they were stealing it from the powder room then. Because we worked at grenades. So there was a powder room that they had the-- the explosive, that they put into the grenade. So from that, they were taking out the powder and giving it to the men somehow. This was all-- probably that nobody knew, you know. It wasn't spread out through the-- through the barracks or through the people, that everybody should know what's going on. That's why I didn't know, and I wasn't interested to know what's going on. So that's what happened to-- to those four girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came January 18th, the dead march. We went to work. And somebody came over to me. You know the way to go home? That the Russians are already in Zawiercie. I says, how would I know a way to run back home? I says, I have no idea. That the civilian master told her that the Russians are already in our home town. And we probably-- that all Birkenau is evacuated. And we're going to be evacuated from Auschwitz tonight. So we came back to the blocks. There was no food. There were thousands of-- tens of thousands of people. And we marched out at night in cold and snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The jets were-- the-- the bombers were-- were flying. We didn't know who they are-- English, American, or Russian. And we walked at night, the whole night, until we came to a village. We slept with pigs just to warm up. And we walked. That was two or three days. We didn't know where we going in cold. And the snow was coming in-- till we came to Gleiwitz. And in Gleiwitz, this was the dead march. And in Gleiwitz, we went into the open cattle cars with snow, with ice on the bottom. And they told you to stay or to sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in like 100 in a-- in a wagon, crumbled in. We couldn't stay. We couldn't sit. We couldn't turn. With a open car, the snow was coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- the train was rolling through Germany. We didn't know where we going-- till somebody jumped up, like somebody was holding her. It was a station. And she says, oh, my God. It's Berlin-- a-- a girl that was from Germany. But the train was rolling-- I don't know-- day/night. And it stopped. And they say, everybody out. And it was Ravensbruck. This was January, maybe 21st or so. Because the 18th, we left for two, three days. Who know-- January 25th.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Ravensbruck. And I says, this is the second worst camp. Because I heard of Ravensbruck in Auschwitz. The lady, that mom, in the factory that we worked, cleaned the toilets. She was a German prisoner. They send her from Ravensbruck to Auschwitz already, to give her a job. And she says, this is the worst. I says, my God, where did I go with-- from leaving Auschwitz, coming to Ravensbruck? Now, do you know where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Ravensbruck was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace in Mecklenburg-- Mecklenburg. This is the-- the-- the part of Germany. And we came in. And it was snow. Maybe-- maybe 80,000 people arrived that-- that few days. Everybody was coming from Auschwitz to Ravensbruck. And there was no room where to get in there. So we stayed outside in the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then somebody walks around and goes, where are the children? Where are the children? Where are the children? So I got up. I says, here I am. I says, what can happen? You know, I'm here already sitting in the snow the whole night. So my girlfriends-- I had three-- two girlfriends with me. And they were older-- that we stuck together. So she says, I'm coming with you. I says, OK. So she says she's a child. And she's a-- they took us to the sauna.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us to take a shower. They gave us a hot soap. And they put us-- let us sleep in-- in the sauna. This was my lucky-- also my lucky day. And the next day, they located us in a block. We stayed in there. And I says, the first thing that somebody goes out from this camp, I am out. There were tens of thousands of people and no place to be, because it was so crowded. They didn't know what to do with us. They called two days later, that they can-- so whoever wants to go out to another camp. I was the first one with my friends out. And we went to Malchow. It was a quiet camp, also in Mecklenburg. I don't know how far. It wasn't far. I never looked through the map to look where it is. And this was a camp already. We didn't work. They had a few people working. There was no selections. Whoever survived, lived. You lived just to-- just to survive. They took me just for four weeks to work in a factory, also underground. The factory was camouflaged-- and who would to work a little bit for four weeks. And then I came back, and we stayed on the blocks. They gave us food-- a little bit food. The food was rotten, like the bread was stale. It was green. And I couldn't eat green bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a-- a dear friend of mine that she survived with me. She's in Seattle. She ate from my portion, from her portion, the stale part. And she gave me the middle that wasn't stale, because I couldn't-- I couldn't take it somehow. I thought I'm gonna die from it. And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freida-- Freida Hall. And she ate the stale food for the whole time. Because they brought the bread fresh, and they put it in a storage room until it got stale. And then they served it to us. And we stayed there till May 1st without working-- just hanging, just laying around without-- without any purpose in life. The Red Cross came once. And they took people to Sweden. So they took my two friends that I went to school with. So I wanted to go with them. I ran. They cut off just in front of me. And I says, those are my sisters. No way. They took them to Sweden-- maybe 200 people. And I remained there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And like, May 1st, they brought in a lot of Muselmann people-- men with blankets-- naked, from another camp. And we looked at them. I says, what's going on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is the war to-- and we didn't even know if the war is going to end. But we looked at those men, the naked, skin and bone men-- as you see it-- with the blankets. And they stayed in our camp. And May the 2nd, they say, everybody out from the camp. They are liquidating the camp. And we are marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we packed up. And we were marching with the German SS. There was no kapos anymore, no block altesters anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were marching. And the people who are staying behind-- and whoever couldn't march was falling. The Germans didn't shoot anymore. And we-- we marched through a city. So the Germans said we were left-- just a-- a small group of people. And I was-- we were five people holding together-- my-- my girlfriends from my hometown. And the Germans says, if you come though this town with us, we let you free. And we passed the city, because the German were running away already. They knew what's coming. And so we passed the city, because they were afraid they will take them to the army, with the army. So he says, after the city, they will run, and we will run. So after the-- passing the city, they let us go. And we were just on our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met-- we saw two Polacks with boxes of Red Cross packages. So we got very friendly, because we had nothing what to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went into a field close to the road. And they opened the packages. And they gave us to eat. And we ate the whole thing up. And then they say the Americans are like 20 kilometers from this side, and the Russians are 30 from this side. And we are in the middle. So we started going towards the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parchim-- Parchim. Also, this was in Mecklenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]
